coredata.dc:description: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) pollution poses a significant global environmental challenge, with current photocatalysts exhibiting limited efficiencies under natural sunlight, especially in neutral to alkaline conditions or at high Cr(VI) concentrations. In this study, amine-rich biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from peanut shells and ethylenediamine via a hydrothermal method, combined with titanium dioxide (TiO2) to form a composite with a narrower band gap. This enhanced the electrostatic interactions with Cr(VI), thus improving photocatalytic performance. The composite (1 % CDs) effectively reduced 50 mg/L of Cr(VI) within 60 min under sunlight irradiation, demonstrating stable performance across various pH levels (3, 5, 7, and 9) and Cr(VI) concentrations (10 to 60 mg/L). Even after five cycles, the reduction efficiency remained high at 96.83 %. The composite also achieved reduction rates of 90.09 % and 89.01 % for domestic and electroplating wastewater, respectively, while exhibiting antibacterial properties. Free-radical scavenging experiments confirmed that electrons and holes were the primary active species driving the reduction. This work highlights the potential of the composites for efficient and stable Cr(VI) reduction in real-world water treatment applications.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient reduction of Cr(VI) from industries sewage using novel biomass-driven carbon dots modified TiO2 under sunlight

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) pollution poses a significant global environmental challenge, with current photocatalysts exhibiting limited efficiencies under natural sunlight, especially in neutral to alkaline conditions or at high Cr(VI) concentrations. In this study, amine-rich biomass-derived carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from peanut shells and ethylenediamine via a hydrothermal method, combined with titanium dioxide (TiO2) to form a composite with a narrower band gap. This enhanced the electrostatic interactions with Cr(VI), thus improving photocatalytic performance. The composite (1 % CDs) effectively reduced 50 mg/L of Cr(VI) within 60 min under sunlight irradiation, demonstrating stable performance across various pH levels (3, 5, 7, and 9) and Cr(VI) concentrations (10 to 60 mg/L). Even after five cycles, the reduction efficiency remained high at 96.83 %. The composite also achieved reduction rates of 90.09 % and 89.01 % for domestic and electroplating wastewater, respectively, while exhibiting antibacterial properties. Free-radical scavenging experiments confirmed that electrons and holes were the primary active species driving the reduction. This work highlights the potential of the composites for efficient and stable Cr(VI) reduction in real-world water treatment applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial sewage from processes including electroplating, metal polishing, tanning leather, steel production, and photography frequently contains hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) [1]. The discharge of Cr(VI) ions into aquatic and terrestrial environments poses severe toxic risks, adversely affecting aquatic life and soil microorganisms. Human exposure to Cr(VI) can result in numerous health complications, including respiratory issues and skin ulcers. Due to its carcinogenic, mutagenic, and teratogenic properties, Cr(VI) presents a significant threat to both public health and the ecological environment. Therefore, the development of cost-effective and efficient methods for Cr(VI) reduction is a critical focus in current research. Various techniques have been explored for Cr(VI) reduction, including ion exchange [2], bioremediation [3], electrodialysis [4], membrane separation [5], adsorption [6], chemical precipitation [7], and photocatalysis [8]. Among these, photocatalytic reduction is particularly attractive due to its straightforward, clean, and convenient approach.

Photocatalysis offers an effective method for Cr(VI) reduction by harnessing solar radiation to generate electrical energy [9]. This process involves redox reactions initiated by photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) within the conduction and valence bands (VB) of the photocatalyst, respectively, leading to the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Specifically, superoxide radicals (•
O
2
-
) can be produced from O2 molecules in a solution by e−, with a strong reducing power. Concurrently, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) are produced when h+ interacts with H2O and OH−. These ROS migrate to the surface of the photocatalyst, playing an essential role in the reduction of Cr(VI) [10]. Therefore, effective photocatalysts should exhibit high light absorption and quick conversion rates for real-world applications [11]. Although current studies have demonstrated the promising photocatalytic performances of various catalysts, several challenges hinder the effective photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) including the following: (1) Low reduction efficiency at high concentrations of Cr(VI), (2) limited performance under neutral and alkaline conditions, and (3) experiments mainly involving laboratory light sources using visible and UV light, which restricts the applicability of the method under natural sunlight in real-world environments. Therefore, it is crucial to develop photocatalysts that function effectively across a wide pH range and can efficiently reduce high concentrations of Cr(VI) in the presence of natural sunlight.

Carbon dots (CDs) are an innovative class of fluorescent nanocarbon materials that have become essential in photocatalysis due to their high stability, ease of synthesis, and exceptional optical and electrical properties [12]. By integrating CDs with semiconductor photocatalysts, the photocatalytic efficiency is increased as a result of the improved adsorption capabilities, large increase in light-absorption range, and decrease in bandgap energy [13]. Biomass is increasingly recognized as an important precursor for CDs, owing to its abundance of heteroatoms which facilitate self-doping, resulting in CDs with superior properties [14]. As renewable and eco-friendly resources, biomass and waste materials have garnered significant attention [15]. Biomass waste, including peanut shells, celluloses, lignin, and proteins, are considered an excellent option for fabricating CDs owing to their abundance, accessibility, environmental friendliness, and low cost [16]. Utilizing biomass waste represents a practical and environmentally conscious strategy for producing CDs, aligning with sustainable development goals.

Building on this background, using a hydrothermal technique, we first created positively charged RN-CDs (Scheme 1 ). These RN-CDs were then used to alter TiO2, resulting in a reduced bandgap energy and the introduction of positive charges, which enhance electrostatic interactions between the photocatalyst and Cr(VI). Under a range of circumstances, such as varying RN-CD concentrations, photocatalyst doses, pH levels, and initial Cr(VI) concentrations, the effectiveness of RN-CDs/TiO2 in reducing Cr(VI) was assessed. The stability and practical applicability of RN-CDs/TiO2 were confirmed via ion interference and Cr(VI) reduction tests. Furthermore, water samples from sewage treatment and electroplating plants were utilized to assess the reduction capabilities of RN-CDs/TiO2 in real-world polluted water scenarios. To clarify the photocatalytic mechanism, studies involving radical trapping were carried out. In this study, we present a novel and straightforward photocatalyst that offers a technical solution for Cr(VI) reduction across a broad range of pH levels and concentrations, providing a viable method for cleaning up contaminated water.
Text S1 contains a thorough explanation of the substances and materials used in this investigation.

RN-CDs were successfully synthesized from peanut-shell powder using a modified one-pot hydrothermal approach described in a previous study [17]. First, 30.0 mL of deionized water containing peanut-shell powder (0.5 g) and ethylenediamine (EDA; 200 μL) was sonicated. After being moved to a 50-mL stainless-steel autoclave walled with polytetrafluoroethylene, the mixture was heated for 5 h at 180 °C. To get rid of the bigger particles, the mixture was centrifuged for 30 min at 4000 rpm after it cooled to room temperature. Once gathered, the supernatant was run through a membrane filter with a 0.22-µm opening. After that, the resultant solution was purified by dialyzing it via a membrane with a 500 Da molecular weight cut-off for 8 h in deionized water. The final solid RN-CDs powder was obtained after freeze-drying.

TiO2 solutions were mixed with solid RN-CDs (1, 2, and 3 %) to create RN-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites. The resulting mixtures were thoroughly stirred in 30.0 mL of suspension for 1 h. The mixtures were stirred, then placed in a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined high-pressure autoclave. There, they underwent a 6 h hydrothermal treatment at 60 °C. The mixes underwent treatment, centrifugation, and two rounds of ethanol and deionized water washing. The final RN-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were obtained by freeze-drying the cleaned material for 48 h.

A 300 W xenon lamp (PLS-SCE300D, Beijing, China) was employed as the light source to simulate natural sunlight for the photoreduction of Cr(VI). In the experimental setting, 100 mL of a 50 mg/L Cr(VI) solution was mixed with 0.2 g of the RN-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst. To attain adsorption–desorption equilibrium, the mixture was agitated for 30 min in the dark before the photocatalytic reaction began. After then, the xenon lamp was turned on while staying roughly 15 cm away from the solution's surface. For analysis, samples were taken out of the Cr(VI) solution on a regular basis. After sampling, the photocatalyst was separated from the solution using a 0.22-μm membrane filter. The residual Cr(VI) concentration was determined via spectrophotometry and the diphenyl carbazide (DPC) colorimetric method (Text S2). Furthermore, the influence of various environmental factors on the Cr(VI) photoreduction efficiency was investigated, including the photocatalyst dosage, initial Cr(VI) concentration, pH level, and RN-CD content. To further verify the antibacterial performance of the photocatalyst, Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were selected as model strains, and antibacterial experiments were conducted (Text S3).

The stability of the photocatalyst was tested through five cycles of reduction tests in order to determine the application potential of RN-CDs/TiO2. Additionally, Tests for photocatalysis were carried out under natural sunlight and in the presence of ion interference to examine its practical effectiveness. The total monthly solar radiation for the experimental period is shown in Fig. S14. The total daily solar radiation is 1314.48 W/m2. In order to guarantee the accuracy and repeatability of the findings, all photocatalytic experiments were carried out in triplicate under consistent conditions. This approach minimized random variations and provided robust data for analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection, treatment, and experimentation with sewage samples
ce:section.ce:para: Wastewater samples were collected using high-density polyethylene (HDPE) buckets and a string. After transferring the samples into a 20-L HDPE container and ensuring no headspace, the lid was securely sealed. To maintain a low temperature, the container was transported to the laboratory in a cool box filled with ice. Upon arrival, the samples were stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator for preservation. Samples of influent and effluent were obtained from the same sewage treatment plant (Urumqi Hexi Water Service Co., Ltd.), with the relevant data listed in Table S1. Furthermore, sewage samples from an electroplating plant in Urumqi city (Fig. S1) were collected, all data for these samples are provided in Table S2. The initial Cr(VI) concentrations for the various sewage were measured (Table S3). Furthermore, ion chromatography (Table S4) and GCMS analyses (Table S5) were conducted to obtain detailed chemical profiles of the samples. The chemical oxygen demand (COD) of the sewage samples was determined using a Thermo Fisher Orion 8006cX COD analyzer (USA), while the total organic carbon (TOC) was measured using a Mettler Toledo 6000TOCi sensor (USA).

The collected water samples were centrifuged and filtered to remove impurities before experimentation. To assess the photocatalytic efficacy, sewage samples were pretreated with 10 mg/L of Cr(VI). The impact of various dilution levels on the photocatalytic performance was evaluated for both influent and effluent samples. In these experiments, 0.2 g/100 mL of RN-CDs/TiO2 was added, and photocatalytic tests were conducted on the original, 5-fold diluted, and 10-fold diluted influent and effluent samples under controlled laboratory conditions. Further, five cyclic photocatalytic tests were performed with both influent and effluent samples to evaluate the stability of RN-CDs/TiO2 in domestic sewage. To assess the catalytic performance of RN-CDs/TiO2 in practical environments, photocatalytic experiments were also conducted under natural sunlight using influent, effluent, and electroplating sewage samples, each spiked with 10 mg/L of Cr(VI).

As this study involved sewage samples from domestic sources, the growth trend of E. coli was used as a criterion to evaluate the performance of the photocatalytic disinfection. Influent water samples with and without a photocatalyst were used in the photocatalytic experiments and 0.1 mL samples were taken every 30 min for plate coating. An incubator was set to alternate between 12 h of light and 12 h of darkness at a temperature of 37 °C. The coated plate was turned upside down and cultured in an incubator for 48 h. The disinfection performance of the photocatalyst was evaluated by observing the growth morphology of E. coli.
The detailed characterization methods for the photocatalysts are described in detail in Text S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structural characteristics of the photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the RN-CDs, TiO2, and RN-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite microstructures are shown in Fig. 1 . The RN-CD particles exhibit an average size of ∼ 6.57 nm and a lattice spacing of ∼ 0.28 nm, determined from the analysis of roughly 100 particles (Fig. 1a). Fig. 1b presents the crystalline structure of TiO2, revealing a lattice spacing of 0.35 nm, characteristic of the anatase phase of TiO2 [18]. Fig. 1c displays the high-resolution TEM images of RN-CDs/TiO2, with overlapping lattices of TiO2 (0.35 nm) and RN-CDs (0.28 nm) observed in certain regions (Fig. 1d). Further analysis was performed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and elemental mapping to investigate the elemental composition of the RN-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite (Fig. 1e). The elemental distributions of C, N, Ti, and O within the composite are shown in Fig. 1f–i, demonstrating effective incorporation of the hydrothermally synthesized RN-CDs onto the TiO2 nanoparticle surfaces.

The optical properties of RN-CDs under various illumination conditions were examined (Fig. S2). In ambient light, aqueous solutions of RN-CDs appear colorless, however, they exhibit strong blue fluorescence under UV light at wavelengths of 312 and 365 nm, with maximum excitation at 390 nm and peak emission at 451 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was utilized to examine the chemical composition and bonding structures of RN-CDs. The XPS survey spectrum indicates that RN-CDs are primarily composed of 67.92 % carbon (C), 24.64 % oxygen (O), and 7.43 % nitrogen (N) (Fig. S3). The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra (Fig. S4) show three significant peaks at 286.34 eV (C–O/C–N bonds), 284.80 eV (C–C bonds), and 283.32 eV (C=C/C=N bonds) [19,20]. The high-resolution O 1s spectra (Fig. S5), combined with the FTIR spectrum, reveal peaks at 531.52 eV for C–OH/C–O–C bonds and 529.91 eV for OLattice bonds [20,21], suggesting the presence of hydroxyl groups that contribute to the favorable water solubility of CDs [22]. In the high-resolution N 1s spectra (Fig. S6), the peaks at 400.07 and 398.16 eV correspond to pyrrolic N and pyridinic N bonds, respectively [23–27]. These findings confirm the successful synthesis of N-doped CDs through hydrothermal methods. The presence of many functional groups containing nitrogen on the surface of CDs can impart a positive charge to CDs [19], which may enhance the adsorption of TiO2 with Cr(VI). Moreover, studies have demonstrated that the presence of various functional groups on the surface of CDs containing C, N, and O can improve their photophysical and chemical properties, facilitating charge or energy transfer with pollutants in solution [28]. Furthermore, the XPS spectra for TiO2 and 1 % RN-CDs/TiO2 are presented in Fig. S7 and Fig. 6, respectively. The modification of TiO2 with RN-CDs resulted in a slight shift of the stretching vibration peaks. This shift suggests that the incorporation of RN-CDs altered the surface chemical environment of TiO2, causing changes in the XPS peak positions.

Fig. 2 a presents the Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of TiO2, RN-CDs, and 1 % RN-CDs/TiO2. Characteristic peaks at 3364.33, 3355.96, and 3247.59 cm−1 are attributed to O–H and N–H bonds, indicating good water solubility [29,30]. Peaks at 2929.89 and 2927.15 cm−1 correspond to C–H bonds. Distinctive peaks at 1637.10, 1636.52, and 1574.93 cm−1 are associated with C–N/C=N bonds [31]. In the RN-CDs/TiO2 composite [32], an extra C–H stretching vibration peak at 2927.15 cm−1 shows that RN-CDs were successfully incorporated onto the TiO2 matrix.

X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analyses (Fig. 2b) indicated that the diffraction pattern of RN-CDs/TiO2 similarly matches that of pure TiO2, suggesting that the modification with CDs did not significantly affect the phase structure of TiO2. The diffraction peaks of pure TiO2 and the RN-CDs/TiO2 composite are well-aligned, appearing at 25.64°, 38.34°, 48.91°, 54.98°, 56.14°, 64.02°, 70.09°, 71.98°, and 76.72°, corresponding to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220), and (215) crystal planes, respectively [33,34]. Notably, the diffraction peaks of RN-CDs/TiO2 exhibit a slight shift to lower angles, which may be attributed to interactions between RN-CDs and TiO2 that modify the lattice characteristics of TiO2 in a subtle way.

Fig. 2c shows that the zeta potential of pure TiO2 is 1.86 mV, which increases to 3.35 mV with the addition of RN-CDs, indicating a rise in its positive charge due to the functional groups on the composite’s surface. Interaction with Cr(VI), which is negatively charged and highly soluble in water, leads to a decrease in the positive charge on the RN-CDs/TiO2 surface. This reduction in positive charge weakens the electrostatic interactions between Cr(VI) and RN-CDs/TiO2, facilitating the adhesion of Cr(VI) to the photocatalytic active sites of the material [35]. The reduction experiment results corroborate these observations and are consistent with the zeta-potential measurements.

The optical properties of TiO2 and the 1 % RN-CDs/TiO2 composite were assessed using ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS). The light absorption of pure TiO2 is at ≤ 396.82 nm, whereas the RN-CDs/TiO2 composite shows enhanced light absorption extending to 412.91 nm, attributable to the incorporation of RN-CDs (Fig. 2d). The bandgap energies (E g) of both materials were determined through further analysis (Fig. 2e), using Eqs. (1) and (2) [36].

where α represents the optical absorption coefficient, A is a constant related to the transition probability, c (3.0 × 108 m⋅s−1) denotes the speed of light, h (6.626 × 10−34 J⋅Hz−1) is Planck’s constant, and v is the radiation frequency. The E g value for pure TiO2 is 3.25 eV, which is slightly higher than that measured for the RN-CDs/TiO2 composite (3.13 eV).

Fig. 2f presents the XPS results focused on the VB spectrum of TiO2 (2.04 eV) and RN-CDs/TiO2 (2.49 eV). Additionally, the conduction band (CB) energy of the RN-CDs/TiO2 composite (E CB = −0.64 eV) was calculated using the Mulliken electronegativity theory, as shown in Eq. (3).

This approach offers detailed insights into the electronic structures and energy levels of the composite materials. Given that the bandgap of RN-CDs/TiO2 is narrower than that of pure TiO2, it enables electron activation at lower energy levels (i.e., longer wavelengths). This feature underscores the potential of RN-CDs/TiO2 for photocatalytic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of the RN-CDs/TiO2 composite
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of the RN-CDs/TiO2 composite for Cr(VI) reduction was evaluated under simulated sunlight using a xenon lamp (excitation wavelength > 365 nm) [37]. Compared to dark conditions, RN-CDs/TiO2 exhibited significant Cr(VI) reduction after 1 h of sunlight irradiation (Fig. S8). The photocatalytic activity of the RN-CDs/TiO2 composites outperformed that of pure TiO2. Notably, increasing the RN-CD doping level to 3 % further enhanced their reduction efficiency (Fig. 3 a). After 60 min, the Cr(VI) reduction efficiency for 1 % RN-CDs/TiO2 was 98.61 %, while for 2 % and 3 % RN-CDs/TiO2, it was 95.94 % and 95.66 %, respectively. This improvement indicates that a higher RN-CD content provides additional adsorption sites, thereby improving the interaction with pollutant ions and demonstrating the composite's potential for effective photocatalytic applications.

The photocatalyst reduction kinetics followed the pseudo-first-order model (Eq. (4)).
(4)
- ln ( C /
C 0
) = k t
where k (min−1) represents the kinetic rate constant, and C 0 and C are the initial and residual Cr(VI) concentrations at time t, respectively. Fig. 3b presents the linear kinetics of Cr(VI) reduction by various photocatalysts, demonstrating a strong correlation with the pseudo-first-order kinetic model. The 1 % RN-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst exhibited the highest reduction rate constant (0.06388 min−1), significantly outperforming the other photocatalysts (Table S6). This improvement is attributed to the distinctive properties of RN-CDs, including their superior photon conversion and charge-transfer capabilities. Consequently, the photocatalyst with 1 % RN-CDs/TiO2 was identified as the most effective for this reaction.

The impact of photocatalyst dosage (0–0.3 g/100 mL) on the reduction efficiency was investigated (Fig. 3c). Increasing the dosage significantly improved the reduction efficiency, achieving 100 % reduction rates at dosages of 0.2 and 0.3 g/100 mL. Analysis using the primary power model showed that at 0.2 g/100 mL, the reduction rate was 0.06388 min−1, comparable to the rate at 0.3 g/100 mL (0.06922 min−1; Fig. 3d; Table S7). Although the 0.3 g/100 mL dosage exhibited a slightly higher reduction rate, the difference between the two dosages was minimal, with both achieving complete reduction within 60 min. Therefore, for a balance between effectiveness and practicality, the 0.2 g/100 mL 1 % RN-CDs/TiO2 dosage was chosen for further analyses.

The reduction curves for the Cr(VI) solutions at different starting pH levels (3, 5, 7, and 9) are shown in Fig. 3e. The RN-CDs/TiO2 composite effectively reduced Cr(VI) to completion within 60 min across both acidic and alkaline conditions. This high efficiency is attributed to the positive charge on RN-CDs/TiO2, which enhances electrostatic interactions with Cr(VI), facilitating its migration to the catalytic surface even in alkaline conditions. The first-order kinetic rates for Cr(VI) reduction at different pH values are presented in Fig. 3f. At pH 7, the reduction rate is 0.06771 min−1, which is slightly higher than those at pH 3 and 5. Furthermore, at pH 9, the reduction rate reaches 0.08177 min−1 (Table S8). These results demonstrate the effectiveness of 1 % RN-CDs/TiO2 in reducing Cr(VI) across a broad pH range.

It also examined at how the initial Cr(VI) concentrations (10–60 mg/L) affected the reduction efficiency. Fig. 3g shows that the time required for Cr(VI) reduction increased with higher initial concentrations under constant conditions. The reduction kinetics for different Cr(VI) concentrations were analyzed using the first-order dynamic equations (Fig. 3h; Table S9). The reduction rate at the lowest concentration (10 mg/L) was notably higher compared to higher concentrations, with a progressive decline in the rate of decline as the concentration of Cr(VI) raised. This trend suggests that elevated Cr(VI) concentrations limit photon penetration into the photocatalyst, reducing the number of photoactive species and increasing competition for the catalytic sites [38]. Despite this, RN-CDs/TiO2 demonstrated excellent reduction capabilities even at higher concentrations, underscoring its effectiveness for Cr(VI) reduction.

The consistency and adaptability of 1 % RN-CDs/TiO2 are crucial for assessing their cost-effectiveness and environmental sustainability. To test the practical applicability of RN-CDs/TiO2, we conducted five regeneration cycles. RN-CDs/TiO2 retained a high Cr(VI) reduction rate of 96.83 % even after five cycles, highlighting its exceptional reusability (Fig. S9). Fig. 2a, Fig. 2b and Fig. 6 respectively present the FT-IR, XRD and XPS spectra, respectively, of the RN-CDs/TiO2 before and after recycling. It is evident that the functional groups of the RN-CDs/TiO2 composites exhibit minimal changes after the multiple cycles. The characteristic absorption peaks remain intact, indicating that the chemical environment of the materials does not undergo significant alterations. This stability suggests that the RN-CDs/TiO2 composites retain their structural integrity and functional properties even after repeated use, highlighting their potential for practical applications in photocatalysis.

To assess the adsorption capacity of RN-CDs/TiO2, We contrasted it using TiO2 alone as a control group and studied the variations in equilibrium adsorption capacity (Q e) with the initial Cr(VI) concentration for both materials. The results revealed that at the same initial Cr(VI) concentrations, RN-CDs/TiO2 exhibited a higher Q e compared to TiO2 (Fig. 4 a). This suggests that the incorporation of CDs significantly enhances the adsorption capacity for Cr(VI). Additionally, we applied two widely recognized isotherm models, the Langmuir (Eq. (5)) and Freundlich (Eq. (6)) models to examine the adsorption behavior in more detail [39,40].
(5)
Q e
=
Q
max
×
K L
×
C e
1 +
K L
×
C e
(6)
Q e
=
K F
×
C e
1 / n
where Q max represents the theoretical maximum adsorption capacity, K L and K F are the Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption constants, respectively, while n denotes the heterogeneity factor. The linear regression coefficients (R 2) were determined by fitting the experimental data shown in Fig. 4b and 4c. The adsorption parameters listed in Table S10 indicate that the Freundlich model affords the highest R 2 values, suggesting that the adsorption behaviors of RN-CDs/TiO2 and TiO2 toward Cr(VI) are better described by a multilayer adsorption process [41]. Additionally, the Langmuir model estimates that the Q max of RN-CDs/TiO2 is 11.31 mg/g, which is 1.56 times higher than that for TiO2 (7.23 mg/g). The impact of contact time on the adsorption capacity is demonstrated in Fig. 4d. Demonstrating that the presence of RN-CDs increases up Cr(VI) adsorption, the adsorption capacity (Q t) of RN-CDs/TiO2 exceeds that of TiO2 at the same contact time (t). Eq. (7), which is a pseudo-first-order model, was utilized to explain the adsorption kinetics [40].
(7)
Q t
=
Q e
( 1 - e x p
( - k t )
)
where the pseudo-first-order rate constant is denoted by k. Table S11 summarizes the relevant parameters, and Fig. 4e and 4f present the fitting results. The pseudo-first-order model best explains the adsorption of Cr(VI) by RN-CDs/TiO2 and TiO2, according to the R 2 values [42]. This further implies that the photocatalyst's adsorption performance is enhanced by the addition of RN-CDs.

To explore the charge carrier separation and migration behavior in photocatalysts, we performed transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analyses. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, pure TiO2 displayed a relatively low photocurrent intensity, while the RN-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite showed a significant increase in photocurrent. This enhancement reflects a marked improvement in the charge carrier separation and migration efficiency [43,44]. Our results indicate that the incorporation of RN-CDs effectively minimizes e––h+ pair recombination, thereby greatly enhancing the photocatalytic performance of the nanocomposite. The EIS data in Fig. 5b further support these findings. In the EIS plot, each arc corresponds to the resistance encountered during charge transfer, with a smaller arc radius indicating lower charge transfer resistance [45]. The RN-CDs/TiO2 nanocomposite exhibits a smaller arc radius compared to pure TiO2, indicating lower charge transfer resistance and more efficient charge separation [46].

The photocatalytic experiment results clearly demonstrate that the addition of RN-CDs greatly improves the Cr(VI) reduction efficiency of TiO2 photocatalysts in aqueous solutions. This improvement is mainly due to the reduced band gap (E g) of the composite material, which extends its light absorption range [47]. In particular, the addition of RN-CDs modifies TiO2′s light absorption edge, enhancing its capacity to harvest light (Fig. 2d). Compared to pure TiO2, the RN-CDs/TiO2 composite exhibits a smaller band gap (Fig. 5c), allowing for more effective absorption of solar energy. During the photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI), the incidence of high-energy photons induces the photocatalyst to generate e––h+ pairs, followed by the separation of the photogenerated charges, with e− transitioning to CB and leaving h+ in VB. On the surface of RN-CDs/TiO2, the e− react with O2 to produce •
O
2
-
, while the h+ react with H2O to generate •OH [48]. Compared with pure TiO2, RN-CDs/TiO2 not only exhibits a stronger photoelectric response but also a lower charge transfer resistance, indicating its ability to generate more photogenerated e––h+ pairs that participate in the reaction, thus further enhancing its reduction performance for Cr(VI) (Fig. 5d).

To further validate the generation and role of these active species, we conducted electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments to detect the free radicals produced during the photocatalytic process. The 1:1:1:1 and 1:2:2:1 intensities of the characteristic quartets are associated with the 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline n-oxide (DMPO)–•
O
2
-
and DMPO–•OH signals, respectively (Fig. 5e and 5f). By employing 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine 1-oxyl free radical (TEMPO) to capture h+ and e– (Fig. 5g and 5 h), a reduction in the characteristic signals of both h+ and e– indicates the involvement of photogenerated e––h+ pairs in the photocatalytic reaction. According to the EPR results, the photocatalytic process mainly produces e–, h+, •OH, and •
O
2
-
. To reveal which one plays the leading role, active-species trapping experiments were conducted. •
O
2
-
is captured by p-benzoquinone (BQ), •OH is captured by isopropyl alcohol (IPA), h+ is captured by EDTA-2NA, and e– is captured by EDTA. After the experiments, the contribution of each radical species was determined. The inclusion of the BQ and IPA trapping agents had a minimal impact on the photoreduction reaction (Fig. 5i and 5j). Even with an increase in the concentration of trapping agents, the Cr(VI) reduction efficiency of RN-CDs/TiO2 remained similar. RN-CDs/TiO2 with EDTA-2Na and EDTA exhibited lower reduction rates of 57.73 % and 53.33 %, respectively (Fig. 5k and 5l). With an increase in the dosage of the trapping agents, there was a gradual decrease in the efficacy of RN-CDs/TiO2 in reducing Cr(VI), revealing that photogenerated e––h+ pairs are the dominant active species in the photoreduction of Cr(VI). The above results show that the redox ability of RN-CDs/TiO2 is mainly due to efficient e––h+ pair separation rather than the active species [49].

XPS was utilized to examine the enhanced reduction and photocatalytic synergy mechanisms of RN-CDs/TiO2. The XPS survey spectra of RN-CDs/TiO2 are shown in Fig. 6a. Before the reduction process, RN-CDs/TiO2 contained 35.8 % Ti, 56.72 % O, 7.21 % C, and 0.27 % N. After five reduction cycles, the composition changed to 21.78 % Ti, 58.07 % O, 19.15 % C, 0.37 % N, and 0.64 % Cr. The presence of Cr after the reduction process suggests that RN-CDs/TiO2 effectively adsorbed Cr particles [50]. In the high-resolution O 1s spectra of RN-CDs/TiO2 (Fig. 6b), the deconvoluted peaks at 530.13 and 529.01 eV, corresponding to Ti–O and Ti=O bonds, respectively, were observed prior to the photocatalytic process. Ti–O bonds are typically associated with the bulk structure, while Ti=O bonds are indicative of terminal oxygen species or surface defects [51]. After the photocatalytic reaction, a shift in both peaks was observed, with the Ti–O peak moving to 530.05 eV and the Ti=O peak shifting more significantly to 528.50 eV [52]. The decrease in binding energy, particularly the significant shift of the Ti=O peak, suggests that partial oxygen reduction may have occurred during the photocatalytic process [53]. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra are presented in Fig. 6c. Three peaks were observed at 287.20 eV (C–N), 284.80 eV (C–C), and 283.35 eV (C=C/C=N) [54,55]. After photocatalytic reduction, these peaks shifted lower, indicating ionic bonding between the carbon functional groups (such as hydroxyl groups) and Cr(III) [56]. Fig. 6d shows the high-resolution Cr 2p XPS spectra of RN-CDs/TiO2. Peaks corresponding to Cr 2p1/2 and Cr 2p3/2 are observed at approximately 586 and 576 eV, respectively. Additionally, the peak at 586 eV was fitted as a double peak with components at 586.17 eV (Cr(VI)) and 584.79 eV (Cr(III)). Similarly, the peak at 576 eV has two components at 578.58 eV (Cr(VI)) and 576.09 eV (Cr(III)) [57]. Such changes confirm the reduction of Cr(VI) [50]. Fig. 6e presents the N 1s spectra of RN-CDs/TiO2. Peaks observed at 399.76 and 399.01 eV before the reaction are attributed to pyrrolic N and pyridinic N bonds, respectively [20]. The shift of the N peak indicates that the reduction process of RN-CDs/TiO2 to Cr(VI) involves electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding [58]. Additionally, the Ti 2p spectra of the RN-CDs/TiO2 composites (Fig. 6f) show four peaks at 463.29 eV (Ti4+ 2p1/2), 457.77 eV (Ti4+ 2p3/2), and 457.56 eV (Ti3+ 2p3/2) before the reaction [58–60]. Three peaks are observed at 463.01 eV (Ti4+ 2p1/2), 458.50 eV (Ti3+ 2p1/2), and 457.25 eV (Ti4+ 2p3/2) after the reaction. Simulations suggest that e––h+ pair recombination may be partially suppressed by the surface oxygen vacancies and Ti3+ species, which would enhance photocatalytic activity [60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic reaction and ion interference under natural sunlight
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess the practical applicability of RN-CDs/TiO2 as a photocatalyst, we carried out photocatalytic reduction tests using Cr(VI) in free-light. The experiments were conducted at 23 °C, 40 % humidity, and a UV index of 5. Using a reaction system with a realistic Cr(VI) concentration of 10 mg/L, 0.2 g of 1 % RN-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst were added. The results indicate that RN-CDs/TiO2 performs exceptionally well under natural sunlight, achieving a 97.12 % reduction in Cr(VI) within just 10 min and complete removal within 30 min. These findings demonstrate the high efficacy of RN-CDs/TiO2 as a photocatalyst for Cr(VI) reduction in practical conditions.

To ensure the catalyst's stability under practical conditions, we assessed the impact of various common ions on the reduction of Cr(VI) under natural sunlight. Ion-interference studies were conducted using 10 mg/L of common metal cations to test the stability and resistance of RN-CDs/TiO2. The results presented in Fig. S10 demonstrate that the presence of various ions, including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Cr3+, Cl–, SO4 2–, and SO3 2–, did not significantly impact the high efficiency of Cr(VI) reduction by the RN-CDs/TiO2 composite. Despite the potential for interference, Cr(VI) was effectively reduced to a considerable extent within 30 min in all cases. Furthermore, the pseudo-first-order kinetic equations corresponding to the various ions' reduction efficiencies are displayed in Fig. S11. The observed linear association (P < 0.05) indicates a good fit between the pseudo-first-order kinetic model and the experimental data. (Table S12), underscoring the high stability of the RN-CDs/TiO2 composite across a range of ion conditions. This phenomenon further highlights the material’s potential for practical applications, particularly in complex aqueous environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance of RN-CDs/TiO2 with industrial water samples
ce:section.ce:para:
All sewage samples contained 10 mg/L of Cr(VI). As shown in Fig. 7
a, within 60 min, TiO2 exhibited reduction rates of 51.54 %, 53.74 %, and 63.85 % for influent samples of domestic sewage at the original, 5-fold dilution, and 10-fold dilution concentrations, respectively, while RN-CDs/TiO2 achieved reduction rates of 80.86 %, 86.31 %, and 88.33 %, respectively. For effluent samples of domestic sewage with three concentration gradients (Fig. 7b), TiO2 achieved reduction rates of 61.04 %, 65.87 %, and 69.82 % within 55 min, while RN-CDs/TiO2 reached reduction rates of 92.25 %, 93.44 %, and 95.56 %, respectively. The photocatalytic performance of TiO2 significantly improved after modification with RN-CDs. Even after five cycles, RN-CDs/TiO2 achieved reduction rates of 72.31 % and 81.67 % for the influent and effluent samples containing 10 mg/L of Cr(VI), respectively (Fig. 7c).

As shown in Fig. 7d, after photocatalytic treatment, the E. coli content in the influent samples of domestic sewage notably decreased following alternating exposure to light and darkness in the incubator, indicating the significant disinfection capability of the prepared RN-CDs/TiO2. To further evaluate the antibacterial efficacy of the 1 % RN-CDs/TiO2 composite, we selected E. coli and S. aureus as model bacteria for photocatalytic antibacterial experiments. As shown in Fig. 7e and 7f, in the control group, after 30 min of dark reaction and 60 min of continuous illumination, the bacterial colony density did not show any significant changes, indicating that the experimental conditions of temperature and light intensity had no noticeable impact on bacterial growth. Upon introducing the 1 % RN-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst, although the bacterial density did not significantly decrease during the dark reaction phase (30 min), the antibacterial effect was markedly enhanced under illumination. Specifically, pure TiO2 had little effect on bacterial colony density under light, while the 1 % RN-CDs/TiO2 composite significantly inhibited bacterial growth. This suggests that while the antibacterial effect of 1 % RN-CDs/TiO2 is limited under dark conditions, it can effectively inhibit the growth of E. coli under light irradiation by generating a large amount of ROS through photocatalysis [61]. Furthermore, since both E. coli and S. aureus are negatively charged and RN-CDs are positively charged, the introduction of CDs increases the positive charge of TiO2, facilitating the more effective contact and interaction between TiO2 and the bacteria, thereby significantly enhancing the antibacterial capability of the composite material [19].

Moreover, in outdoor sunlight photocatalytic experiments, RN-CDs/TiO2 achieved reduction rates of 79.04 % for influent samples of domestic sewage within 90 min, and 90.09 % for effluent samples of domestic sewage within 60 min (Fig. S12). Even in electroplating sewage with high salt concentrations, RN-CDs/TiO2 achieved a reduction rate of 89.01 % for 10 mg/L Cr(VI) within 45 min (Fig. S13). The three-dimensional fluorescence maps (Fig. 7g and 7 h) of the raw solution of the influent domestic sewage and their contour plots demonstrate the distribution of fluorescence intensity, while Fig. 7i and 7j show the fluorescence profiles of the solution after the reaction with RN-CDs/TiO2. The highest fluorescence intensity in the untreated water samples is observed at excitation wavelengths of 300–400 nm and emission wavelengths of 400–500 nm. After photocatalytic treatment, the fluorescence intensity of the sewage samples was significantly reduced, indicating that during the photocatalytic process, the structure of some organic matter, such as UVC-like humic substances [62], aromatic compounds, and oxygen-enriched compounds [63], is disrupted. Specifically, the characteristic fluorescence peaks in the raw water samples indicate the presence of significant amounts of humic substances and aromatic compounds, which have a strong fluorescence response in the ultraviolet region (UVC). The photocatalytic tests showed that the RN-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst effectively degrades organic pollutants. The significant decrease in fluorescence intensity indicated that these substances are effectively oxidized and decomposed via photocatalysis. In addition, the weakening of the fluorescence intensity indicates a reduction in the concentration of the organic substances, accompanied by structural changes, especially the destruction of aromatic structures and oxygen-rich functional groups. This further verifies the effectiveness of the photocatalytic process, indicating that RN-CDs/TiO2 effectively degrades and removes organic pollutants from sewage to improve sewage quality. Such results demonstrate the significant photocatalytic performance and treatment efficacy of the RN-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst in practical applications.

A comparison of the reaction conditions and efficiencies of various photocatalytic materials (Table S13) confirmed the superior photocatalytic performance of RN-CDs/TiO2. Most materials were tested with initial Cr(VI) concentrations of 10–25 mg/L and achieved satisfactory reduction performance. TiO2 nanotube arrays sensitized by Cd0.8Zn0.2S nanoparticles [63] achieved a modest 74 % decrease of 50 mg/L Cr(VI) within 120 min. In contrast, RN-CDs/TiO2 afforded a full reduction of 50 mg/L Cr(VI) in only 60 min under solar irradiation, demonstrating exceptional efficacy, even at high Cr(VI) levels. Furthermore, even at low concentrations, RN-CDs/TiO2 maintained a competitive advantage in reduction efficiency. For Cr(VI) concentrations of 10 and 25 mg/L, Qi et al. [50] and Tu et al. [21] achieved complete reduction within 105 and 120 min, respectively. In contrast, RN-CDs/TiO2 achieved a corresponding reduction within 20 min. Additionally, the majority of studies exhibited high reduction rates exclusively under acidic conditions. In contrast, RN-CDs/TiO2 demonstrated good performance over a wide pH range, achieving a remarkable reduction rate of 100 % even under alkaline conditions. Finally, in contrast to the laboratory light sources used in other studies, the experiments with RN-CDs/TiO2 were conducted under conditions closer to those encountered in practical applications (i.e., natural sunlight). In summary, RN-CDs/TiO2 exhibited outstanding performance for high-concentration Cr(VI) pollution with high reaction rates and activity over a wide range of pH conditions, as well as high efficacy under natural sunlight illumination. Coupled with the environmentally friendly and accessible extraction of RN-CDs from peanut shells, the RN-CDs/TiO2 composite photocatalyst has substantial practical value.

In this study, we developed a novel RN-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst by incorporating amine-rich biomass-derived CDs, resulting in a significantly lower bandgap energy and enhanced positive charge compared to pure titanium dioxide. This modification facilitates the improved separation of photogenerated charges and extends the light absorption spectrum, leading to stronger electrostatic interactions with Cr(VI). The RN-CDs/TiO2 photocatalyst demonstrated exceptional performance, achieving complete reduction of 50 mg/L Cr(VI) within 60 min, while maintaining a high efficiency across various concentrations and pH levels (3–9). Under natural sunlight, it completely reduced 10 mg/L Cr(VI) in just 30 min, indicating its practical applicability. The catalyst exhibited excellent recyclability, structural stability, resistance to ion interference and antibacterial properties. Additionally, RN-CDs/TiO2 showed significant Cr(VI) reduction rates and effective disinfection capabilities across different sewage concentrations. Overall, this study presents a viable approach for enhancing photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction in diverse environmental conditions, offering valuable insights and methodologies for effective water treatment.
